Framework · free to use

How to train your team on Claude.

Buying Claude seats does not change how your team works. Training does — if it's the right training. Most internal AI training fails the same way: a 60-minute generic demo, polite nodding, then no behavior change. This is the playbook for training that actually sticks.

By Bill Colbert · Founder, Treetop Growth Strategy
Published May 2026 · More from the library
The principle

Train on workflows, not features

Generic AI training ("here's what an LLM is, here's how prompting works") produces awareness, not adoption. Workflow-specific training ("here is exactly how you, the sales rep, will draft a follow-up email using this Project") produces adoption.

Rule: every training session should leave the participant with one specific workflow they can do that afternoon, faster than they did yesterday.

Week 1

Foundations (90 minutes total)

Session 1: What Claude is and how we use it (30 min)

  • What Claude is, in one slide.
  • What we use it for here (specific workflows we've already built).
  • What we do NOT use it for (data classifications, policy summary).
  • How to access (login, Projects you have access to).

Session 2: Prompting basics (30 min)

  • How to write a clear prompt (with 3 examples in the team's actual workflow context).
  • How to refine when the output is off ("more concise," "add an objection-handling section," "shorter").
  • How to use Claude Projects vs the standalone chat.

Session 3: First real workflow, hands-on (30 min)

  • Pick the most relevant Project for their role.
  • Walk through one full real-work use, step by step.
  • Have each person do their own example with a real input from their own work.
  • Office hour scheduled for 1 week later.
Week 2-4

Reinforcement & refinement

  • Weekly office hours with the AI lead. Anyone can come; bring real work that is not flowing.
  • Slack channel for prompt sharing. "This prompt worked great" or "Why is Claude refusing X?"
  • Workflow owners check in 1:1 with each team member to see what's actually being used.
  • Update the Project knowledge based on patterns from real use.
Role-specific exercises

What to have each function do

RoleFirst workflowTime to fluency
Sales repDraft a follow-up email from real call notes1-2 sessions
MarketerDraft a content brief from a topic + target keyword2-3 sessions
CSMBuild a QBR pack from usage data + account notes2-3 sessions
ExecutiveGet a one-page brief from a board doc1-2 sessions
OpsSummarize meeting notes into action items + decisions1 session
SupportDraft a reply to a real ticket1-2 sessions
Common failure modes

Where adoption stalls

  • People use Claude once, get a meh output, give up. Fix: show them how to iterate. "More concise, shorter, add X" turns a meh draft into a good one in 30 seconds.
  • People use it for the wrong workflows. Fix: be explicit about the workflows where it shines vs the workflows where it does not yet.
  • People do not have time. Fix: assign protected calendar time for the first two weeks. "Use Claude for 30 min/day, every day, for the next 10 work days."
  • People do not trust the output. Fix: review some of their outputs together. Build trust calibration. Show what Claude is reliably good at and what needs human verification.
  • The Project is not loaded with the right knowledge. Fix: iterate. Most Projects need 3-4 rounds of knowledge updates before they hum.
Measuring adoption

How to know it's working

  • Usage frequency: are people using Claude weekly, daily, or never? Daily users adopt; weekly users sometimes; never-users will never absent intervention.
  • Workflow output: are proposals/content/replies actually shipping faster, with the AI tool?
  • Voluntary sharing: are people posting prompts and patterns in the Slack channel without being asked? That's the strongest signal of organic adoption.
  • 1:1 check-ins: ask each person, "what's the workflow you use Claude for most often?" If they can name one, they're adopted. If they can't, they're not.
